text: Upon entering the abdominal cavity, the surgeon dissects adhesions between the gastric remnant and the major omentum. Short gastric vessels are identified and divided, allowing mobilization of the stomach up to the gastroesophageal junction.
The surgeon visually dissects the gastric remnant, pouch, and alimentary limb. Upon identifying the stenosis area, the distal part of the gastric remnant is transected.
The surgeon elevates the gastric remnant, ensuring the left gastric artery remains intact to preserve blood supply to the gastric pouch.

The surgeon uses a harmonic scalpel to dissect adhesions attached to the gastric remnant, revealing the area of the gastrojejunostomy. The gastrojejunostomy is clearly identified and divided, along with the distal part of the gastric remnant, in a single block.

The surgeon gains control of the gastric remnant and identifies the left gastric artery on the pouch. Using UL-II, the gastroesophageal junction is identified, and the pouch is transected proximal to the stenotic and penetrated gastrojejunostomy.
The surgeon uses green cartridge linear staplers to transect thickened gastric tissue, which has been affected by previous surgeries and chronic inflammation.

The surgeon lyses adhesions between the alimentary limb and the mesenterium, allowing the limb to be repositioned to the upper abdomen in an anti-colic, anti-gastric manner without tension. The staple lines are oversewn using running vial sutures.

The surgeon reinforces all staple lines and reconstructs the upper portion of the gastrointestinal tract by performing a side-to-side gastroenterostomy. Clip-up pliers are used to enhance hemostasis during the procedure.

The surgeon performs oversawing on the gastric pouch, addressing dilatations on both the gastric remnant and pouch. The procedure involves multiple dilatations, typically not exceeding five, before considering revision of the anastomosis.

The surgeon performs an enterotomy at the midpoint between the mesenterium and the anti-mesenteric border of the small bowel. Using a blue cartridge linear stapler, a side-to-side gastroenterostomy is created on the posterior wall, assisted by an E-wall tube. The gastrotomy is completed with the linear stapler, and the anterior wall is closed using a double layer of rendezvous sutures.
The surgeon performs a full thickness running suture, starting from the proximal end and moving distally, then returning to lock the distal part of the suture to the proximal part.
